1.source:
Siquis in hoc artem populo non novit amandi,
     Hoc legat et lecto carmine doctus amet.
1.translation: Let anyone who lacks the art of love read on,  And having read, his ignorance is gone!

2.source:
Arte citae veloque rates remoque moventur,
     Arte leves currus: arte regendus amor.
2.translation: Let anyone who lacks the art of love read on,  And having read, his ignorance is gone! It’s art that makes a ship respond to sail and oar,

3.source:
Curribus Automedon lentisque erat aptus habenis,               
     Tiphys in Haemonia puppe magister erat:
Me Venus artificem tenero praefecit Amori;
     Tiphys et Automedon dicar Amoris ego.
3.translation: It’s art that makes a ship respond to sail and oar,  And art that drives a team or guides Amor. Automedon controlled his reins with expert grip,

4.source:
Ille quidem ferus est et qui mihi saepe repugnet:
     Sed puer est, aetas mollis et apta regi.
4.translation: Now Venus says that I’m to rule her tender son;  I’ll be Love’s Tiphys and Automedon. And though he’s wild, and far too prone to fight with me,

5.source:
Phillyrides puerum cithara perfecit Achillem,
     Atque animos placida contudit arte feros.
5.translation: And though he’s wild, and far too prone to fight with me, He’s just a boy; I’ll win out easily.  (The way that Chiron broke Achilles on a lyre,

6.source:
Qui totiens socios, totiens exterruit hostes,
     Creditur annosum pertimuisse senem.
6.translation: (The way that Chiron broke Achilles on a lyre, By banking down that youngster’s feral fire). They say that he whom enemies and friends both feared,

7.source:
Quas Hector sensurus erat, poscente magistro               
     Verberibus iussas praebuit ille manus.
7.translation: They say that he whom enemies and friends both feared,  Cowered before a Centaur’s hoary beard. Those hands that Hector felt, Achilles had to hold

8.source:
Aeacidae Chiron, ego sum praeceptor Amoris:
     Saevus uterque puer, natus uterque dea.
8.translation: Those hands that Hector felt, Achilles had to hold   Out to the lash; he did as he was told. Achilles, Love: two pupils. Chiron, me: two teachers.

9.source:
Sed tamen et tauri cervix oneratur aratro,
     Frenaque magnanimi dente teruntur equi;               
Et mihi cedet Amor, quamvis mea vulneret arcu
     Pectora, iactatas excutiatque faces.
9.translation: Achilles, Love: two pupils. Chiron, me: two teachers.  Two sons of goddesses, both savage creatures. The bull endures the yoke; upon his neck it sits.

10.source:
Quo me fixit Amor, quo me violentius ussit,
     Hoc melior facti vulneris ultor ero:
Non ego, Phoebe, datas a te mihi mentiar artes,               
     Nec nos aeriae voce monemur avis,
Nec mihi sunt visae Clio Cliusque sorores
     Servanti pecudes vallibus, Ascra, tuis:
Usus opus movet hoc: vati parete perito;
     Vera canam: coeptis, mater Amoris, ades!
10.translation: So Love succumbs, although his arrow pierce my heart.  Let him burn his brand or shoot his dart; The deeper that he wounds, the hotter that wound burns,

11.source:
Este procul, vittae tenues, insigne pudoris,
     Quaeque tegis medios, instita longa, pedes.
11.translation: No. Life informs these lines, so heed this well-versed bard Who sings the truth. O Venus, guide and guard!  And stay away, thin ribbons—signs of modesty—

12.source:
Nos venerem tutam concessaque furta canemus,
     Inque meo nullum carmine crimen erit.
12.translation: And stay away, thin ribbons—signs of modesty—  And flounces hiding feet we mustn’t see. I sing safe, sanctioned love and love affairs in rhyme

13.source:
Principio, quod amare velis, reperire labora,               
     Qui nova nunc primum miles in arma venis.
13.translation: I sing safe, sanctioned love and love affairs in rhyme  That advocates no error, sin, or crime. First, those of you who come to love as raw recruits

14.source:
Proximus huic labor est placitam exorare puellam:
     Tertius, ut longo tempore duret amor.
14.translation: First, those of you who come to love as raw recruits   Must find some girl your lover’s fancy suits. Next, work to win the one who pleases you the best.

15.source:
Hic modus, haec nostro signabitur area curru:
     Haec erit admissa meta terenda rota.
15.translation: Next, work to win the one who pleases you the best.  Then, make that love endure time’s toughest test. This is my bound, and this my chariot’s well-marked ground;

16.source:
Dum licet, et loris passim potes ire solutis,
     Elige cui dicas 'tu mihi sola places.'
16.translation: This is my bound, and this my chariot’s well-marked ground; This is the turnpost it will wheel around.  Not yet reined in, footloose and free to roam and run,

17.source:
Haec tibi non tenues veniet delapsa per auras:
     Quaerenda est oculis apta puella tuis.
17.translation: Not yet reined in, footloose and free to roam and run,  Pick someone you can call “my only one.” She won’t come from thin air, straight down from clear blue skies,

18.source:
Scit bene venator, cervis ubi retia tendat,               
     Scit bene, qua frendens valle moretur aper;
Aucupibus noti frutices; qui sustinet hamos,
     Novit quae multo pisce natentur aquae:
Tu quoque, materiam longo qui quaeris amori,
     Ante frequens quo sit disce puella loco.
18.translation: She won’t come from thin air, straight down from clear blue skies,  This cynosure of love; you’ll need sharp eyes. The hunter knows just where the stag will find his snare,

19.source:
Non ego quaerentem vento dare vela iubebo,
     Nec tibi, ut invenias, longa terenda via est.
19.translation: You, too! Look for substantial love that will go far. But first, you need to know where the girls are!  Now, I won’t tell you to go searching with a sail,

20.source:
Andromedan Perseus nigris portarit ab Indis,
     Raptaque sit Phrygio Graia puella viro,
Tot tibi tamque dabit formosas Roma puellas,               
     'Haec habet' ut dicas 'quicquid in orbe fuit.'
20.translation: Now, I won’t tell you to go searching with a sail, Or send you tramping down some dusty trail. Perseus got his Andromeda from darkest Ind,

21.source:
Gargara quot segetes, quot habet Methymna racemos,
     Aequore quot pisces, fronde teguntur aves,
Quot caelum stellas, tot habet tua Roma puellas:
     Mater in Aeneae constitit urbe sui.
21.translation: But Rome will yield so many pretty girls each day    That “Here is all the world” is what you’ll say. Like full Methymnian vines; like massed Gargarian sheaves;

22.source:
Seu caperis primis et adhuc crescentibus annis,
     Ante oculos veniet vera puella tuos:
Sive cupis iuvenem, iuvenes tibi mille placebunt.
22.translation: Or stars their skies? Far more, the girls who haunt this Rome The Mother of Aeneas calls her home.  If barely blooming beauties set your soul on fire,

23.source:
Cogeris voti nescius esse tui:
Seu te forte iuvat sera et sapientior aetas,               
     Hoc quoque, crede mihi, plenius agmen erit.
23.translation: Then wait: true virgins couldn’t number higher. If it’s young women that you want, a thousand aim To please—and make you doubt which one to claim.

24.source:
Tu modo Pompeia lentus spatiare sub umbra,
     Cum sol Herculei terga leonis adit:
Aut ubi muneribus nati sua munera mater
     Addidit, externo marmore dives opus.
24.translation: Perchance your fancy favors older women? Well,   Trust me. Mere watching makes their numbers swell! Just amble under Pompey’s portico when

25.source:
Nec tibi vitetur quae, priscis sparsa tabellis,
     Porticus auctoris Livia nomen habet:
Quaque parare necem miseris patruelibus ausae
     Belides et stricto stat ferus ense pater.
25.translation: Where one son gave arcades of marble-cladded stone. (His mother added riches of her own.)  And don’t avoid the Empress Livia’s gallery.

26.source:
Nec te praetereat Veneri ploratus Adonis,               
     Cultaque Iudaeo septima sacra Syro.
26.translation: Apollo’s temple, too, where Belus’ daughters stand.  (They kill their men; he threatens, sword in hand.) Adonis, mourned by Venus, also has a shrine,

27.source:
Nec fuge linigerae Memphitica templa iuvencae:
     Multas illa facit, quod fuit ipsa Iovi.
27.translation: Adonis, mourned by Venus, also has a shrine,   And Jewish sites of worship work just fine. Don’t write off Io’s temple, either; linen-wrapped,

28.translation: Don’t write off Io’s temple, either; linen-wrapped, Jove’s heifer draws them till, like her, they’re trapped. Even the courts (would you believe it?) suit Love’s sports—

29.source:
amori:
     Flammaque in arguto saepe reperta foro:               
Subdita qua Veneris facto de marmore templo

Illo saepe loco capitur consultus Amori,
     Quique aliis cavit, non cavet ipse sibi:
Illo saepe loco desunt sua verba diserto,               
     Resque novae veniunt, causaque agenda sua est.
29.translation: Jove’s heifer draws them till, like her, they’re trapped. Even the courts (would you believe it?) suit Love’s sports— His torches burning in the heated torts.

30.source:
Hunc Venus e templis, quae sunt confinia, ridet:
     Qui modo patronus, nunc cupit esse cliens.
30.translation: There, often in that place, he finds, beyond belief,   He’s speechless—and his next case is his brief. There Venus in her nearby temple laughs at one

Sed tu praecipue curvis venare theatris:
     Haec loca sunt voto fertiliora tuo.
31.translation: There Venus in her nearby temple laughs at one  Who pled for hire. Now, his case has begun! But theatres are best, where seats curve up in layers;

32.source:
Illic invenies quod ames, quod ludere possis,
     Quodque semel tangas, quodque tenere velis.
32.translation: But theatres are best, where seats curve up in layers; These places set the scene for answered prayers.  You’ll find there one to love, or one to only play with;

33.source:
Ut redit itque frequens longum formica per agmen,
     Granifero solitum cum vehit ore cibum,
Aut ut apes saltusque suos et olentia nactae

     Copia iudicium saepe morata meum est.
33.translation: You’ll find there one to love, or one to only play with;  Someone to sample, or someone to stay with. An ant will come and go all day, in endless lines,

34.source:
Spectatum veniunt, veniunt spectentur ut ipsae:
     Ille locus casti damna pudoris habet.
34.translation: Well that’s how all the smartest women crowd the shows  In numbers, till my faltering judgment goes. Where they are able to be seen, they come to see—

Primus sollicitos fecisti, Romule, ludos,
     Cum iuvit viduos rapta Sabina viros.
35.translation: Where they are able to be seen, they come to see— A place that’s lethal to all chastity.  Romulus, you made these shows trouble, way back when

39.translation: And rough men clapped (their taste had not yet been refined),  The king gave them the sign for which they pined. At once, they jumped up, clamor making passion clear,

Ut fugiunt aquilas, timidissima turba, columbae,

Atque ita 'quid teneros lacrimis corrumpis ocellos?

     Haec mihi si dederis commoda, miles ero.
44.translation: Saying,“Why spoil your tender eyes with tears? I’ll be A husband like your mother’s was, you see?”   Romulus, you knew how to pay your soldiers right;

45.source:
Scilicet ex illo sollemnia more theatra
     Nunc quoque formosis insidiosa manent.
45.translation: Romulus, you knew how to pay your soldiers right; Pay me that way and I’ll enlist tonight! Clearly, from that our current playhouse mores came . . .

Nec te nobilium fugiat certamen equorum;

46.translation: Clearly, from that our current playhouse mores came . . . A trap for beauties stage shows still remain. Remember gallant horses, too (I mean the races):

Pallia si terra nimium demissa iacebunt,

52.translation: This wins a prize (if she permits): you get the chance  To give her legs a momentary glance. There’s more: be sure you know whose knees those are one row

Parva leves capiunt animos: fuit utile multis

53.translation: There’s more: be sure you know whose knees those are one row Behind: her soft back isn’t where they go.  The simple mind will find delight in trifles: much

54.translation: The simple mind will find delight in trifles: much May come from cushions lent an artful touch.   Also, a little fan can help to keep her cool;

     Sparsaque sollicito tristis harena foro.
55.translation: Also, a little fan can help to keep her cool; Then prop her tender feet up with a stool. Yes, look at all the Circus offers . . . and the Forum—

56.source:
Illa saepe puer Veneris pugnavit harena,               
     Et qui spectavit vulnera, vulnus habet.
56.translation: Yes, look at all the Circus offers . . . and the Forum—  Whose wretched floor can also draw a quorum. For Love has often battled on its bloody sands,

Dum loquitur tangitque manum poscitque libellum

57.translation: For Love has often battled on its bloody sands,   Inflicting wounds on those safe in the stands. He asks to see a program, touches what’s inviting,

Quid, modo cum belli navalis imagine Caesar

58.translation: And then . . . Love’s arrow. Deeply wounded, he Becomes like victims that he’s come to see.  And didn’t Caesar re-enact that sea-fight (when

59.translation: And didn’t Caesar re-enact that sea-fight (when The Greeks and Persians fought)? That’s when young men  And girls came flocking into Rome. From coast to coast

60.translation: And girls came flocking into Rome. From coast to coast   They came, till all the world called Rome its host. Who didn’t find some love in all that surging mass?

61.source: Eheu, quam multos advena torsit amor!
61.translation: They came, till all the world called Rome its host. Who didn’t find some love in all that surging mass?   How many fell for foreign girls—alas!

62.source:
Ecce, parat Caesar domito quod defuit orbi
     Addere: nunc, oriens ultime, noster eris.
62.translation: Who didn’t find some love in all that surging mass?   How many fell for foreign girls—alas! See now how Caesar looks to add what empire lacks:

Parthe, dabis poenas: Crassi gaudete sepulti,
     Signaque barbaricas non bene passa manus.
63.translation: See now how Caesar looks to add what empire lacks:  The far Far East will soon enjoy our Pax. Parthia, you’ll pay, while buried Crassi shall exult.

Ultor adest, primisque ducem profitetur in annis,
     Bellaque non puero tractat agenda puer.
64.translation: Parthia, you’ll pay, while buried Crassi shall exult. Our eagles rise you thought you could insult.  “Vengeance is here,” young Gaius cries. And so, no more

Parcite natales timidi numerare deorum:
     Caesaribus virtus contigit ante diem.
65.translation: “Vengeance is here,” young Gaius cries. And so, no more  A boy, he goes to wage a grown man’s war. Stop counting birthdays of the gods, you cowards, you!

66.source:
Ingenium caeleste suis velocius annis               
     Surgit, et ignavae fert male damna morae.
66.translation: Stop counting birthdays of the gods, you cowards, you!  Caesars show courage long before they’re due. Great god-like power comes faster than the years can bring,

67.source:
Parvus erat, manibusque duos Tirynthius angues
     Pressit, et in cunis iam Iove dignus erat.
67.translation: Great god-like power comes faster than the years can bring,   And thinks delay a damned ignoble thing. Tirynthian Hercules crushed snakes barehanded when

Nunc quoque qui puer es, quantus tum, Bacche, fuisti,
     Cum timuit thyrsos India victa tuos?
68.translation: Tirynthian Hercules crushed snakes barehanded when  A tot, already Jove-like even then. Bacchus, a boy still, how big were you when you bashed

     Nunc iuvenum princeps, deinde future senum;

Induit arma tibi genitor patriaeque tuusque:
     Hostis ab invito regna parente rapit;
Tu pia tela feres, sceleratas ille sagittas:
     Stabit pro signis iusque piumque tuis.
70.translation: Avenge these brothers’ wounds; you’ve brothers of your own.   A son yourself, defend a father’s throne. Your father—father of your country—arms you now;

71.source:
Vincuntur causa Parthi: vincantur et armis;
     Eoas Latio dux meus addat opes.
71.translation: You’ll carry pious pikes, they’ll shoot their shameful darts; Your eagles all will stand by righteous hearts.  The Parthians’ cause is lost; then let them lose by war.

72.source:
Marsque pater Caesarque pater, date numen eunti:
     Nam deus e vobis alter es, alter eris.
72.translation: The Parthians’ cause is lost; then let them lose by war.  My Prince, add eastern wealth to Latium’s store!  Our fathers, Mars and Caesar: lend him all your power

73.source:
Auguror, en, vinces; votivaque carmina reddam,

73.translation: Our fathers, Mars and Caesar: lend him all your power   (One god and one god-at-some-future-hour). Listen! Gaius will conquer and I’ll sing those deeds

Consistes, aciemque meis hortabere verbis;
     O desint animis ne mea verba tuis!
74.translation: Listen! Gaius will conquer and I’ll sing those deeds   To glorify his name as time recedes. Oh, you will stand, and with my words, exhort your men, Gaius.

75.source:
Tergaque Parthorum Romanaque pectora dicam,
     Telaque, ab averso quae iacit hostis equo.
75.translation: Oh, you will stand, and with my words, exhort your men, Gaius.  I pray they may not fail you then. My verse will be of Roman fronts and Parthian backs—

76.source: Qui fugis ut vincas, quid victo, Parthe, relinquis?
76.translation: My verse will be of Roman fronts and Parthian backs— Cowards who fire from horses making tracks.  What’s left in your defeat if you “retreat to win”?

77.source: Parthe, malum iam nunc Mars tuus omen habet.
77.translation: Cowards who fire from horses making tracks.  What’s left in your defeat if you “retreat to win”?  O Parthian, that’s a bad way to begin.

78.source:
Ergo erit illa dies, qua tu, pulcherrime rerum,
     Quattuor in niveis aureus ibis equis.
78.translation: What’s left in your defeat if you “retreat to win”?  O Parthian, that’s a bad way to begin. So there will come a day when Gaius, shining bright,

79.source:
Ibunt ante duces onerati colla catenis,

79.translation: So there will come a day when Gaius, shining bright,  Rides golden with his four-horse team in white. Then, Parthian chiefs will stagger on, necks bowed by chains.

80.source:
Spectabunt laeti iuvenes mixtaeque puellae,
     Diffundetque animos omnibus ista dies.
80.translation: Then, Parthian chiefs will stagger on, necks bowed by chains.   They’ll never flee again across the plains. That day, young joyous men and girls will revel so,

Atque aliqua ex illis cum regum nomina quaeret,

81.translation: That day, young joyous men and girls will revel so,  Their celebrating hearts will overflow. “What peak is that?” she’ll ask.“What land?”“What king of note?”

Hic est Euphrates, praecinctus harundine frontem:

82.translation: Tell her everything, and not just if you’re bid;  If you don’t know, respond as if you did: “This one’s Euphrates, wreathed with reeds; and that one there

83.translation: “This one’s Euphrates, wreathed with reeds; and that one there  Is Tigris with the deep-blue, dripping hair. These are Armenians. Those? They’re Persians, from the race

84.translation: These are Armenians. Those? They’re Persians, from the race   Of Danaë. That? Some quaint Parthian place.” Each one will be some chief; give him his proper name—

174.translation: She swears that it will give her years of sheer delight.  She’s “got” to have it, since “the price is right.” Plead lack of cash, she’ll take your note. Give up the fight—

175.source:
Quid, quasi natali cum poscit munera libo,
     Et, quotiens opus est, nascitur illa, sibi?
175.translation: Plead lack of cash, she’ll take your note. Give up the fight— And curse the day you ever learned to write.  How can she claim a birthday cake five times a year?

176.source:
Quid, cum mendaci damno maestissima plorat,
     Elapsusque cava fingitur aure lapis?
176.translation: How can she claim a birthday cake five times a year? Who would have thought that birthdays came so near?   What do you do when she pretends her dear friend’s died?

177.source:
Multa rogant utenda dari, data reddere nolunt:
     Perdis, et in damno gratia nulla tuo.
177.translation: What do you do when she pretends her dear friend’s died? She’s lost an earring! All last night, she cried.  So many things you’ll never see again, girls “borrow,”

178.source:
Non mihi, sacrilegas meretricum ut persequar artes,               
     Cum totidem linguis sint satis ora decem.
178.translation: So many things you’ll never see again, girls “borrow,” But they don’t care; you “lend” things to your sorrow. Ten months, ten tongues, and still they wouldn’t be enough

179.source:
Cera vadum temptet, rasis infusa tabellis:
     Cera tuae primum conscia mentis eat.
179.translation: Ten months, ten tongues, and still they wouldn’t be enough  To tell of women’s ruses—not by half. Let letter-tablet wax go first; it may convey

180.source:
Blanditias ferat illa tuas imitataque amantem
     Verba; nec exiguas, quisquis es, adde preces.
180.translation: Let letter-tablet wax go first; it may convey The eloquence of what you’re trying to say. Let wax words and endearments dear to lovers go;

181.source:
Hectora donavit Priamo prece motus Achilles;
     Flectitur iratus voce rogante deus.
181.translation: Let wax words and endearments dear to lovers go; Whoever you may be, your prayers should flow.  Achilles, touched by pleas, gave Priam back his son;

182.source: Promittas facito: quid enim promittere laedit?
182.translation: Achilles, touched by pleas, gave Priam back his son;  By praying voices, vengeful gods are won. Make promises! They do no harm, so who can chide us?

183.translation: By praying voices, vengeful gods are won. Make promises! They do no harm, so who can chide us? In promises, each man can be a Midas.

184.source:
Spes tenet in tempus, semel est si credita, longum:               
     Illa quidem fallax, sed tamen apta dea est.
184.translation: Make promises! They do no harm, so who can chide us? In promises, each man can be a Midas. Just once have faith in Hope, and she’ll have faith in you—

185.source:
Si dederis aliquid, poteris ratione relinqui:
     Praeteritum tulerit, perdideritque nihil.
185.translation: Just once have faith in Hope, and she’ll have faith in you—  A cheater, but . . . a useful goddess, too. The trouble is, you give, and she no longer heeds you;

186.source:
At quod non dederis, semper videare daturus:
     Sic dominum sterilis saepe fefellit ager:               
Sic, ne perdiderit, non cessat perdere lusor,
     Et revocat cupidas alea saepe manus.
186.translation: The trouble is, you give, and she no longer heeds you; She loses nothing and no longer needs you.  Give nothing; always seem about to be a donor.

187.source:
Hoc opus, hic labor est, primo sine munere iungi;
     Ne dederit gratis quae dedit, usque dabit.
187.translation: The gambler goes on losing so he won’t have lost. The dice entice the hand from which they’re tossed.  “This is the task; this is the work”: gift-free success.

188.source:
Ergo eat et blandis peraretur littera verbis,               
     Exploretque animos, primaque temptet iter.
188.translation: “This is the task; this is the work”: gift-free success. To save what’s given, she’ll give more, not less! So scratch a billet-doux, then send it off to sound

189.source:
Littera Cydippen pomo perlata fefellit,
     Insciaque est verbis capta puella suis.
189.translation: So scratch a billet-doux, then send it off to sound  Her out (and be the first to tread that ground).  Cydippe tricked herself when, unawares, she read

190.source:
Disce bonas artes, moneo, Romana iuventus,
     Non tantum trepidos ut tueare reos;               
Quam populus iudexque gravis lectusque senatus,
     Tam dabit eloquio victa puella manus.
190.translation: Cydippe tricked herself when, unawares, she read Aloud, repeating what an apple said. Roman young men, learn your trade; the liberal arts

191.source:
Sed lateant vires, nec sis in fronte disertus;
     Effugiant voces verba molesta tuae.
191.translation: As judge, or populace, or Senate yields to you,  So eloquence will win a woman, too. But hide your art: don’t play the highbrow.

192.source: Quis, nisi mentis inops, tenerae declamat amicae?
192.translation: But hide your art: don’t play the highbrow.  Try, instead, To let your language show you’re not well read. Only a dolt would send his girl some stuffed oration.

193.source: Saepe valens odii littera causa fuit.
193.translation: Try, instead, To let your language show you’re not well read. Only a dolt would send his girl some stuffed oration.   (Back come curt letters in reciprocation.)

194.source:
Sit tibi credibilis sermo consuetaque verba,
     Blanda tamen, praesens ut videare loqui.
194.translation: Only a dolt would send his girl some stuffed oration.   (Back come curt letters in reciprocation.) Plain, ordinary words create a sense of trust,

195.source:
Si non accipiet scriptum, inlectumque remittet,
     Lecturam spera, propositumque tene.
195.translation: Plain, ordinary words create a sense of trust, As if you’re there. But coaxing’s still a must. She sends your letter back, unread? Don’t sit and mope,

196.source:
Tempore difficiles veniunt ad aratra iuvenci,
     Tempore lenta pati frena docentur equi:
Ferreus adsiduo consumitur anulus usu,
     Interit adsidua vomer aduncus humo.
196.translation: She sends your letter back, unread? Don’t sit and mope, But persevere—and never give up hope.  In time, unwilling cattle plough without a fight;

197.source: Quid magis est saxo durum, quid mollius unda?
197.translation: An iron ring thins down from constant wear and tear, And constantly the ground grinds down the share. What’s harder than a rock? What’s softer than a wave?

198.source: Dura tamen molli saxa cavantur aqua.
198.translation: And constantly the ground grinds down the share. What’s harder than a rock? What’s softer than a wave?  Still, gentle water hollows out the cave.

199.source:
Penelopen ipsam, persta modo, tempore vinces:
     Capta vides sero Pergama, capta tamen.
199.translation: What’s harder than a rock? What’s softer than a wave?  Still, gentle water hollows out the cave. Hard work could win Penelope (well, who can tell?);

200.translation: Hard work could win Penelope (well, who can tell?); We know that Troy fell late—and yet it fell. She’s read your blandishments but won’t send back her note?

201.source:
cogere noli:
     Tu modo blanditias fac legat usque tuas.
201.translation: We know that Troy fell late—and yet it fell. She’s read your blandishments but won’t send back her note? Don’t fret. At least she read the words you wrote.

202.source:
Quae voluit legisse, volet rescribere lectis:
     Per numeros venient ista gradusque suos.
202.translation: She’s read your blandishments but won’t send back her note? Don’t fret. At least she read the words you wrote.   Whoever wants to read will write back in the end.

203.source:
Forsitan et primo veniet tibi littera tristis,
     Quaeque roget, ne se sollicitare velis.
203.translation: Whoever wants to read will write back in the end. It’s bit-by-bit on which you can depend. At first, some sullen note may “ beg you to desist”;

204.source:
Quod rogat illa, timet; quod non rogat, optat, ut instes;               
     Insequere, et voti postmodo compos eris.
204.translation: At first, some sullen note may “ beg you to desist”; She may write back,“You really must resist.” In truth, she fears you will, while hoping that you won’t.

205.source:
Interea, sive illa toro resupina feretur,
     Lecticam dominae dissimulanter adi,
Neve aliquis verbis odiosas offerat auris,
     Qua potes ambiguis callidus abde notis.
205.translation: In truth, she fears you will, while hoping that you won’t.  Go on! You’ll never win her if you don’t. Meanwhile, if she is being carried on her chair,

206.source:
Seu pedibus vacuis illi spatiosa teretur
     Porticus, hic socias tu quoque iunge moras:
Et modo praecedas facito, modo terga sequaris,
     Et modo festines, et modo lentus eas:
Nec tibi de mediis aliquot transire columnas               
     Sit pudor, aut lateri continuasse latus;
Nec sine te curvo sedeat speciosa theatro:
     Quod spectes, umeris adferet illa suis.
206.translation: If someone’s there—don’t let yourselves be overheard; With two-faced words, your meaning can be blurred.  Or if her aimless feet go wandering some arcade,

207.source:
Illam respicias, illam mirere licebit:
     Multa supercilio, multa loquare notis.
207.translation: Don’t let her sit in theatres alone, but go . . .  And view her more-than-lovely cameo. It’s fine to look, but finer to appreciate.

208.source:
Et plaudas, aliquam mimo saltante puellam:
     Et faveas illi, quisquis agatur amans.
208.translation: It’s fine to look, but finer to appreciate. (There’s much a gesture, much a brow, can state.)  Clap when some mincing dancer mimes the ingenue;

209.source:
Cum surgit, surges; donec sedet illa, sedebis;
     Arbitrio dominae tempora perde tuae.
209.translation: Clap when some mincing dancer mimes the ingenue;  Applaud whoever plays the lover, too.   If she gets up, get up; and if she sits, you sit.

210.source:
Sed tibi nec ferro placeat torquere capillos,               
     Nec tua mordaci pumice crura teras.
210.translation: If she gets up, get up; and if she sits, you sit. Your time is hers; her wish is wasting it. I don’t advise a curling iron for your hair,

211.source:
Ista iube faciant, quorum Cybeleia mater
     Concinitur Phrygiis exululata modis.
211.translation: I don’t advise a curling iron for your hair,   Or pumicing your legs until they’re bare. That’s for the Magna Mater singers, chanting odes

212.source:
Forma viros neglecta decet; Minoida Theseus
     Abstulit, a nulla tempora comptus acu.
212.translation: That’s for the Magna Mater singers, chanting odes  Or ululating in the Phrygian modes. For men, slight grooming’s best; when Theseus won the fair

213.source:
Hippolytum Phaedra, nec erat bene cultus, amavit;
     Cura deae silvis aptus Adonis erat.
213.translation: For men, slight grooming’s best; when Theseus won the fair Ariadne, no pin or clip held back his hair.  Unkempt Hippolytus won Phaedra; Myrrha’s boy,

214.source:
Munditie placeant, fuscentur corpora Campo:
     Sit bene conveniens et sine labe toga:
Lingula ne rigeat, careant rubigine dentes,               
     Nec vagus in laxa pes tibi pelle natet:
Nec male deformet rigidos tonsura capillos:
     Sit coma, sit trita barba resecta manu.
214.translation: Unkempt Hippolytus won Phaedra; Myrrha’s boy,  Adonis, forest-born, was Venus’ joy. Plain cleanliness works best, and drill-field tans don’t hurt.

215.source:
Et nihil emineant, et sint sine sordibus ungues:
     Inque cava nullus stet tibi nare pilus.
215.translation: Be sure a barber, not a butcher, cuts your hair And trims your beard with care. Please try to wear Nails short and clean. Be sure no ugly hair growth shows,

216.source:
Nec male odorati sit tristis anhelitus oris:
     Nec laedat naris virque paterque gregis.
216.translation: Nails short and clean. Be sure no ugly hair growth shows, Sprouting from the hollows of your nose.  Don’t let your breath go sour, and you should take note:

217.source:
Cetera lascivae faciant, concede, puellae,
     Et siquis male vir quaerit habere virum.
217.translation: Don’t let your breath go sour, and you should take note:  Armpits must never smell like billy goat. But any more than that, let wanton girls employ—

218.source:
Ecce, suum vatem Liber vocat; hic quoque amantes               
     Adiuvat, et flammae, qua calet ipse, favet.
218.translation: But any more than that, let wanton girls employ—  Or any man who would prefer a boy. Now Liber calls his bard; he helps keep love alive.

219.source:
Cnosis in ignotis amens errabat harenis,
     Qua brevis aequoreis Dia feritur aquis.
219.translation: Now Liber calls his bard; he helps keep love alive.   The flame with which he burns, he wants to thrive. Crazed Ariadne, wandering down the alien shore

220.source:
Utque erat e somno tunica velata recincta,
     Nuda pedem, croceas inreligata comas,               
Thesea crudelem surdas clamabat ad undas,
     Indigno teneras imbre rigante genas.
220.translation: Crazed Ariadne, wandering down the alien shore  Of little Dia, where the waters roar, Came fresh from having wakened—barefoot, loosely gowned,

221.source:
Clamabat, flebatque simul, sed utrumque decebat;
     Non facta est lacrimis turpior illa suis.
221.translation: She cried out Theseus’ name. The sea was deaf and dumb,   As down soft cheeks, the tears began to come. She cried out and she cried, and both became her well,

222.source:
Iamque iterum tundens mollissima pectora palmis               
     'Perfidus ille abiit; quid mihi fiet?'
222.translation: She cried out and she cried, and both became her well,  Making her charming as the teardrops fell. Fists beat against her tender breasts repeatedly:

223.translation: Fists beat against her tender breasts repeatedly:   “That traitor’s gone. What will become of me? What will become of me?” she cried. Then . . . cymbals crashed!

224.translation: Fists beat against her tender breasts repeatedly:   “That traitor’s gone. What will become of me? What will become of me?” she cried. Then . . . cymbals crashed!

225.source:
ait: sonuerunt cymbala toto
     Litore, et adtonita tympana pulsa manu.
225.translation: “That traitor’s gone. What will become of me? What will become of me?” she cried. Then . . . cymbals crashed!  And everywhere, the thundering drums were bashed.

226.source:
Excidit illa metu, rupitque novissima verba;
     Nullus in exanimi corpore sanguis erat.
226.translation: What will become of me?” she cried. Then . . . cymbals crashed!  And everywhere, the thundering drums were bashed. Her cry half-cried, she fainted from her fear and lay

227.source:
Ecce Mimallonides sparsis in terga capillis:
     Ecce leves satyri, praevia turba dei:
Ebrius, ecce, senex pando Silenus asello
     Vix sedet, et pressas continet ante iubas.
227.translation: Her cry half-cried, she fainted from her fear and lay As pale as if her blood had drained away.  Then . . . look! The Bacchants, hair blown back.

228.source:
Dum sequitur Bacchas, Bacchae fugiuntque petuntque               
     Quadrupedem ferula dum malus urget eques,
In caput aurito cecidit delapsus asello:
     Clamarunt satyri 'surge age, surge, pater.'
228.translation: Behold that sot Silenus on his swaybacked ass,  Grasping its mane, about to hit the grass. He’s after Bacchants, who re-group and circle back.

229.source:
Iam deus in curru, quem summum texerat uvis,
     Tigribus adiunctis aurea lora dabat:               
Et color et Theseus et vox abiere puellae:
     Terque fugam petiit, terque retenta metu est.
229.translation: Then tumbles off old Long-Ears, landing on his head.  “Dad, get up!” cry the Satyrs.“You’re not dead!” Then Bacchus, in a chariot grape vines enfold,

230.source:
Horruit, ut graciles, agitat quas ventus, aristae,
     Ut levis in madida canna palude tremit.
230.translation: Her voice, her color, and all thoughts of Theseus fled.  Three times she tried to flee, but stopped, in dread. A shaken, slender reed, she shuddered in her fears—

231.source:
Cui deus 'en, adsum tibi cura fidelior' inquit:               
     'Pone metum: Bacchi, Cnosias, uxor eris.
231.translation: A shaken, slender reed, she shuddered in her fears—  A slight cane, trembling in the marshy meres. And Bacchus said,“I’m here, and I will care for you.

232.source:
Munus habe caelum; caelo spectabere sidus;
     Saepe reges dubiam Cressa Corona ratem.'
232.translation: And Bacchus said,“I’m here, and I will care for you.   Don’t fear. I’ll do what Theseus couldn’t do. You’ll be a constellation—such your gift shall be.

233.source:
Dixit, et e curru, ne tigres illa timeret,
     Desilit; inposito cessit harena pede:               
Implicitamque sinu (neque enim pugnare valebat)
     Abstulit; in facili est omnia posse deo.
233.translation: You’ll be a constellation—such your gift shall be. The Cretan Crown, you’ll guide ships lost at sea.” He speaks, and lest she fear the beasts, he leaps, and lands

234.translation: He caught her to him (she was weak), then off they drove.  (It isn’t hard when you’re the son of Jove.)   Some sang,“Euhoe”; some“Hail, Hymenaeus,” said.

235.source: Sic coeunt sacro nupta deusque toro.
235.translation: (It isn’t hard when you’re the son of Jove.)   Some sang,“Euhoe”; some“Hail, Hymenaeus,” said. Then bride and god meet on the sacred bed.

236.source:
Ergo ubi contigerint positi tibi munera Bacchi,               
     Atque erit in socii femina parte tori,
Nycteliumque patrem nocturnaque sacra precare,
     Ne iubeant capiti vina nocere tuo.
236.translation: Some sang,“Euhoe”; some“Hail, Hymenaeus,” said. Then bride and god meet on the sacred bed. So when the Dionysian gifts come round to you,

237.source:
Hic tibi multa licet sermone latentia tecto
     Dicere, quae dici sentiat illa sibi:               
Blanditiasque leves tenui perscribere vino,
     Ut dominam in mensa se legat illa tuam:
Atque oculos oculis spectare fatentibus ignem:
     Saepe tacens vocem verbaque vultus habet.
237.translation: Beg the Nyctelian spirits not to let the red  Wine hurt your flirting with a foggy head. Here, you can say so much, from which she may infer

238.source:
Fac primus rapias illius tacta labellis               
     Pocula, quaque bibet parte puella, bibas:
Et quemcumque cibum digitis libaverit illa,
     Tu pete, dumque petis, sit tibi tacta manus.
238.translation: Look deep into her eyes with eyes that seem on fire;  A silent look can speak of great desire. Be sure to be the first to touch the cup her lip

239.source:
Sint etiam tua vota, viro placuisse puellae:
     Utilior vobis factus amicus erit.
239.translation: Whatever food that girl has fiddled with or fingered,  Ask for; be sure your hand on hers has lingered. And try to please your woman’s spouse; make that your end.

240.source:
Huic, si sorte bibes, sortem concede priorem:
     Huic detur capiti missa corona tuo.
240.translation: And try to please your woman’s spouse; make that your end.  He could become quite useful as a friend. Drinking by lot, concede your place, your drinking crown;

241.source:
Sive erit inferior, seu par, prior omnia sumat:
     Nec dubites illi verba secunda loqui.
241.translation: Drinking by lot, concede your place, your drinking crown;  Give him your turn by turning your turn down.  Sitting below (or with you), he should eat before you.

242.source:
Tuta frequensque via est, per amici fallere nomen:               
     Tuta frequensque licet sit via, crimen habet.
242.translation: Sitting below (or with you), he should eat before you. Be sure to let him talk first, though he bore you.  The broad highway? To call yourself a friend, but cheat.

243.source:
Inde procurator nimium quoque multa procurat,
     Et sibi mandatis plura videnda putat.
243.translation: The broad highway? To call yourself a friend, but cheat. The broad highway is sinning’s easy street.  Some agents act in more than you may bargain for,

244.source:
Certa tibi a nobis dabitur mensura bibendi:
     Officium praestent mensque pedesque suum.
244.translation: Some agents act in more than you may bargain for, Watching not just their charges, but far more. How much to drink? The right amount is not quite to

245.source:
Iurgia praecipue vino stimulata caveto,
     Et nimium faciles ad fera bella manus.
245.translation: How much to drink? The right amount is not quite to Where mind—and feet—can’t do what they must do.   Whenever nasty quarrels fuelled by wine begin,

246.source:
Occidit Eurytion stulte data vina bibendo;
     Aptior est dulci mensa merumque ioco.
246.translation: Whenever nasty quarrels fuelled by wine begin,   Be sure you keep your fists from joining in. Eurytion, soaked in wine, was cut down at a stroke.

247.source:
Si vox est, canta: si mollia brachia, salta:               
     Et quacumque potes dote placere, place.
247.translation: Eurytion, soaked in wine, was cut down at a stroke.  At drinking banquets, tell a friendly joke. Sing—if you can; and if your arms are supple, dance.

248.source:
Ebrietas ut vera nocet, sic ficta iuvabit:
     Fac titubet blaeso subdola lingua sono,
Ut, quicquid facias dicasve protervius aequo,
     Credatur nimium causa fuisse merum.
248.translation: Sing—if you can; and if your arms are supple, dance.   If you’ve got talents, please at every chance. Real drunkenness can harm, but sometimes, if you fake it—

249.source:
Et bene dic dominae, bene, cum quo dormiat illa;
     Sed, male sit, tacita mente precare, viro.
249.translation: The world will put down what you say to too much wine, When what you say or do has “crossed the line.”  Try this:“The lady’s health!” Then,“Health to her bed mate,”

250.source:
At cum discedet mensa conviva remota,
     Ipsa tibi accessus turba locumque dabit.
250.translation: Try this:“The lady’s health!” Then,“Health to her bed mate,”  While silent thoughts of him are filled with hate. And when the board’s been cleared, and guests are streaming out,

251.source:
Insere te turbae, leviterque admotus eunti               
     Velle latus digitis, et pede tange pedem.
251.translation: And when the board’s been cleared, and guests are streaming out,  You’ll get the chance you need in all that rout. Join it! Inch up on her as she’s departing. Catch

252.source:
Conloquii iam tempus adest; fuge rustice longe
     Hinc pudor; audentem Forsque Venusque iuvat.
252.translation: Join it! Inch up on her as she’s departing. Catch   Her sleeve, and try to make your footsteps match. The time to talk is now; don’t be some bashful farmer;

253.source:
Non tua sub nostras veniat facundia leges:
     Fac tantum cupias, sponte disertus eris.
253.translation: The time to talk is now; don’t be some bashful farmer;  (Venus, like Chance, will favor the brave charmer).  Don’t let your smooth talk yield to poets’ rules; have just

254.source:
Est tibi agendus amans, imitandaque vulnera verbis;
     Haec tibi quaeratur qualibet arte fides.
254.translation: Don’t let your smooth talk yield to poets’ rules; have just One goal, and talk will come from this self-trust.   Become The Lover; tell her that your heart is aching.

255.source:
Nec credi labor est: sibi quaeque videtur amanda;
     Pessima sit, nulli non sua forma placet.
255.translation: Become The Lover; tell her that your heart is aching. (The tactics have to be of your own making.) But that’s not hard; all women know men want them madly.

256.source:
Saepe tamen vere coepit simulator amare,               
     Saepe, quod incipiens finxerat esse, fuit.
256.translation: But that’s not hard; all women know men want them madly. Even the ugliest believe this gladly. Of course, mock lovers often start to love for real,

257.source:
Quo magis, o, faciles imitantibus este, puellae:
     Fiet amor verus, qui modo falsus erat.
257.translation: Of course, mock lovers often start to love for real,  And end up feeling what they’ve claimed to feel!  Girls, don’t be hard on phony lovers, actors who

258.source:
Blanditiis animum furtim deprendere nunc sit,
     Ut pendens liquida ripa subestur aqua.
258.translation: Girls, don’t be hard on phony lovers, actors who Deceive; today’s false love may yet turn true. The time is now for flattering words to catch her soul—

259.source:
Nec faciem, nec te pigeat laudare capillos
     Et teretes digitos exiguumque pedem:
Delectant etiam castas praeconia formae;
     Virginibus curae grataque forma sua est.
259.translation: The time is now for flattering words to catch her soul— Streams undercutting banks as waters roll.   And don’t stop praising her—her face; her lovely hair;

260.source:
Nam cur in Phrygiis Iunonem et Pallada silvis               
     Nunc quoque iudicium non tenuisse pudet?
260.translation: Even a chaste girl loves to hear you praise her face, And virgins find their beauty full of grace. Why else would Juno and Minerva be mad yet

261.source:
Laudatas ostendit avis Iunonia pinnas:
     Si tacitus spectes, illa recondit opes.
261.translation: Why else would Juno and Minerva be mad yet  About that prize at Troy they didn’t get? The bird of Juno, when he’s praised, will spread his tail

262.source:
Quadrupedes inter rapidi certamina cursus
